coredata.dc:title: 9-N-substituted novel berberine derivative for selective and sensitive nanomolar level fluorometric detection of human hemoglobin: A synthesis, sensing and interaction study
coredata.dc:creator: Nayim, Sk
Jana, Gopal Chandra
Aktara, Mt Nasima
Khatun, Munira
Dhal, Asima
Beg, Maidul
Sahoo, Nandan Kumar
Maji, Anukul
Hossain, Maidul
coredata.dc:description: Quantitative determination of hemoglobin (Hb) level is an obligatory part of diagnostic proceedings and an undisputed health index for a number of chronic diseases. In this study, we have tried to introduce a new 9-N-substituted berberine analogue (BR-N) as a triumphant fluorescent sensor for aptly selective and extremely sensitive nanomolar detection of Hb. The probe was found to endure a rampant quenching in its highly intensified inherent fluorescence emission with successive addition of Hb. Though, the other available proteins and biomolecules were proved to be failure in causing any significant change in fluorescence of BR-N under equivalent experimental condition. Again, the sensitivity of the probe was such that it provided an astonishing detection limit of 0.41 nM having linearity range 1–70 nM. Noteworthy, the presence of Hb caused significant alternation in the nature of the intrinsic fluorescence curve of the probe which was also a differentiating aspect during Hb identification. Experiments revealed that ground state probe-protein complex formation was behind the reduction of fluorescence intensity through static quenching mechanism. The interaction study unearthed the binding constant value to be 2.8 × 105 M−1. Moreover, the probe was also successful when applied in real samples for quantitative Hb determination which spoke for its reliable practical utility in the field of clinical diagnosis from today onwards.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly fluorescent berberine derivative
Hemoglobin detection
Static quenching
Time-correlated single photon counting technique
Real sample application

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of 9-N-substituted berberine derivative (BR-N)
Conformational change of Hb using FTIR spectroscopy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Sk Nayim acknowledges CSIR for Junior Research Fellowship (09/599(0089)/2019-EMR-I). Mt. Nasima Aktara appreciatively acknowledges UGC for MANF Fellowship. We also gratefully acknowledge the USIC, Vidyasagar University for their instrumental facilities and DST-FIST and UGC-SAP programs.

Sk Nayim acknowledges CSIR for Junior Research Fellowship ( 09/599(0089)/2019-EMR-I ). Mt. Nasima Aktara appreciatively acknowledges UGC for MANF Fellowship. We also gratefully acknowledge the USIC, Vidyasagar University for their instrumental facilities and DST-FIST and UGC-SAP programs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectral changes of free hemoglobin (1 μM) and in presence of different concentrations of BR-N (0 to 40 μM). Inset: highlighted spectrum in the region 360–450 nm to consider the spectral variation of the soret band.
Three-dimensional fluorescence (3D) and corresponding contour spectra of BR-N (100 μM) in free state (A, B) and after the addition of Hb (C, D).

Variation of fluorescence intensities on adding different concentrations of Hb into buffered solution of (A) BR-N and (B) its corresponding LOD plot, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of BR-N(100 μM) in absence and presence of different concentration of Hb. Inset of (A) shows the changes under 366 nm UV lamp. λex = 310 nm.

(A) Effect of addition of different biomolecules (proteins/enzymes) into an aqueous buffer solution of BR-N and (B) the corresponding bar plot representation, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of BR-N in absence and presence of different biomolecules (20 μM). λex = 310 nm.
Time resolved fluorescence response of BR-N before and after the addition of Hb.

Panel 1: FTIR spectra in the region of 3500 to 500 cm−1 of (A) free Hb, (B) Hb + 50 μM BR-N, (C) Hb + 100 μM BR-N, (D) Hb + 150 μM BR-N and (E) free BR-N and Panel 2: its deconvoluted spectra of amide-I band (1700–1600 cm−1): A, for free Hb (1000 μM) and its complexes; B, Hb + BR-N (50 μM); C, Hb + BR-N (100 μM); and D, Hb + BR-N (150 μM).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of BR-N (100 μM) at various temperatures in the range 20–80 °C, (B) linear relationship of F0/F versus the temperature over the range from 20 to 80 °C, (C) Temperature dependent emission wavelength at 310 nm excitation, (D) Reversible temperature-dependent fluorescence response of four consecutive cycles between 20 and 80 °C, respectively. λex = 310 nm.
Schematic representation of the synthesis procedure.
Comparison of some reported Hb sensors.
Detection of Hb in human urine samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: 9-N-substituted novel berberine derivative for selective and sensitive nanomolar level fluorometric detection of human hemoglobin: A synthesis, sensing and interaction study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore 721 102, West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Vidyasagar University, Midnapore 721 102, West Bengal, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantitative determination of hemoglobin (Hb) level is an obligatory part of diagnostic proceedings and an undisputed health index for a number of chronic diseases. In this study, we have tried to introduce a new 9-N-substituted berberine analogue (BR-N) as a triumphant fluorescent sensor for aptly selective and extremely sensitive nanomolar detection of Hb. The probe was found to endure a rampant quenching in its highly intensified inherent fluorescence emission with successive addition of Hb. Though, the other available proteins and biomolecules were proved to be failure in causing any significant change in fluorescence of BR-N under equivalent experimental condition. Again, the sensitivity of the probe was such that it provided an astonishing detection limit of 0.41 nM having linearity range 1–70 nM. Noteworthy, the presence of Hb caused significant alternation in the nature of the intrinsic fluorescence curve of the probe which was also a differentiating aspect during Hb identification. Experiments revealed that ground state probe-protein complex formation was behind the reduction of fluorescence intensity through static quenching mechanism. The interaction study unearthed the binding constant value to be 2.8 × 105 M−1. Moreover, the probe was also successful when applied in real samples for quantitative Hb determination which spoke for its reliable practical utility in the field of clinical diagnosis from today onwards.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb) is among the most essential metalloprotein presents in the red blood cells (RBC) of almost all vertebrates and even in some invertebrates. In the RBCs, it is the principal component with dry weight of almost 96%. It is a tetramer of four analogous globin protein subunits and heme subunits. In each heme subunits there is a Fe (II) atom coordinated with protoporphyrin IX ligand [1,2]. One Hb molecule consists of two α units and two β units containing 141 and 146 respective amino acid residues along with 7 and 8 respective numbers of helices [3,4]. In respiratory system, Hb takes part in a crucial role of carrying oxygen from various respiratory organs like lungs, skin, gills etc. to various tissues in the body for metabolic reason; dispersion of hydrogen peroxide, and thereby to allow the electron transfer in between various organs of the body [5,6]. Moreover, it takes away the respiratory wastages like CO2, H+ and 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (BPG) from the working tissues to the lungs and kidneys. The significance of this protein is greater for its striking ability to interact with biologically important CO and NO gases [7,8]. Consequently, normal level of Hb concentration is compulsory for maintaining proper functioning of different body organs [9]. Otherwise a series of fatal and chronic diseases including anemia, leukemia and heart diseases can cause disastrous health concern in human body [10,11]. In diagnostic laboratories measurement of Hb concentration is crucial for being an indicator for diseases like thalassemia, sickle cell disease, kidney diseases, infections, erythrocytosis [2], Malaria [12], polycythemia and post-surgical excessive bleeding [13]. In the blood of a mature man normal concentration of Hb is 13.0 to 18.0 g/dL whereas it is 12.0 to 16.0 g/dL for a mature woman [14]. But worldwide investigations reveal a frightening statistics that more than 2 billion mostly children and women are suffering from anemia today [15]. Thus, exact measurement of Hb content in human blood and urine has become indispensable in modern approach of clinical diagnosis to reflect the human health status. Till date, numerous endeavor have been made including methods like colorimetry [16,17], mass spectrometry [18], spectrophotometry [19–21], spectrofluorimetry [22–24], electrochemistry [25–29], immunoassay [30], chemiluminescence [31–33] and high performance liquid chromatography [34,35]. Although, very few among these are free from limitations like complicated and tedious synthetic procedure, toxic and hazardous raw materials, high cost, water insolubility and higher detection limit. Thus, search for simple, efficient, economical, water soluble Hb sensors with tremendous sensitivity, superb selectivity is still going on.

In our previously reported works berberine derivatives had been designed and successfully applied in the quantitative determination of serum albumin proteins [36,37]. Here, we developed a new 9-N-substituted berberine derivative (BR-N) following a simple, single-step, cost effective, non-toxic, non-hazardous synthesis pathway and effectively employed it for rapid, facile, selective and sensitive determination of Hb. A number of spectrofluorometric along with some spectrophotometric techniques were adapted to examine the stability and the sensitivity of the synthesised probe.The stability of BR-N was judged in respect of time, temperature and pH of the buffer solution. Various modern and updated analysis methodologies including time-correlated single photon counting technique (TCSPC) and three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectroscopy were adapted for an emphatic demonstration of its interaction and sensing behaviour. The binding interaction of BR-N with Hb had been divulged through UV–visible absorption study whereas the binding stoichiometry had been made clear by using Job's plot analysis process. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectral analysis was performed to unravel the effect of BR-N probe on the secondary structure of Hb. The newly synthesised probe was discovered to be capable of identifying Hb with very lower value of detection limit. The exclusive selectivity and extreme sensitivity of the probe speaks for its scientific novelty such that in this modern age of biomedical science this technique can be represented as an innovative and challenging pathway for the quantitative determination of Hb even in the field of application in real sample.

Berberine chloride hydrate was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation (St. Louis, MO, USA). 1-(3-Aminopropyl)imidazole and Hemoglobin human (lyophilized powder) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co, (St. Louis, MO 63103, USA). While the other required chemicals and necessary solvents were merchandised from Merck Pvt. Ltd. (Singapore). All chemicals were put in use as received avoiding any additional purification and all the experiments were executed by thoroughly using Millipore water. All samples were ready in citrate-phosphate (CP) buffer, including 10 mM [Na]+ at pH 7.2. Concentrations were calculated by Ultraviolet-visible absorbance measurements using molar extinction coefficients of 253,368 M−1 cm−1 for Hb at 404 nm, 43,824 M−1 cm−1, 35,700 M−1 cm−1 and 38,940 M−1 cm−1 respectively for BSA, HSA and Lysozyme at 280 nm and 2800 M−1 cm−1 at 319 nm for our probe BR-N.

Mass spectrum of the synthesised probe was executed by MALDI-ToF (VOYGER-DE PRO, Applied Biosystems, USA). Whereas, the NMR study was executed using Bruker Lambda spectrometer (Germany) 1H (400 MHz), 13C {1H} (100 MHz) in CDCl3 solvent using TMS as internal reference at 25 °C. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) analyses were performed with a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum, (Perkin Elmer, USA). The range of spectral region from 4000 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 and the resolution value of 2 cm−1 were chosen for scanning the interferograms. Absorption measurements were completed through a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Kyoto, Japan) with 480 nm/min scan rate and taking quartz cuvettes having path length of 1 cm at 25 °C. Perkin Elmer LS-55 (USA) spectrofluorimeter was exploited to measure each fluorescence spectrum using fluorescence-free 1 cm path length quartz cells. Fluorescence life time experiments were accomplished by a Delta Flex™ Modular Fluorescence Lifetime System (Horiba Scientific, UK) obeying time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique in which a scatterer of a ludox solution was taken as standard and 288 nm excitation source of a delta diode-C1 diode controller was used. In real sample analysis the urine samples were prepared by centrifuging them at 4000 rpm for 10 min and diluting the supernatant part to 100 fold with CP buffer (10 mM, pH 7.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for the synthesis of 9-N-substituted berberine derivative (BR-N)
ce:section.ce:para: For preparing the proposed analogue commercially accessible Berberine chloride was considered as mother-compound. It was allowed to go through a nucleophilic aromatic displacement reaction process with a constituent amine derivative by refluxing in an anhydrous ethanolic solution which resulted in the fabrication of the desired amino-isoquinolinium derivative as a red solid [38,39]. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, 25 °C, TMS) δ = 9.88 (s, 1H), 8.15 (s, 1H), 7.89 (s,1Η), 7.75 (s,1Η), 7.42 (d, 8 Hz, 1Η), 7.02 (s, 1Η), 6.09 (s, 2Η), 5.09 (t, 6 Hz, 2Η), 4.84 (t, 6 Hz, 2Η), 3.94 (s, 3Η), 3.32 (t, 6 Hz, 2Η), 3.16 (t, 6 Hz, 2Η); 13CNMR (100 MHz, CDCl3, 25 °C, TMS), δ 29.65, 31.23, 43.59, 47.79, 55.26, 58.29, 101.96, 103.82, 112.44, 115.28, 119.47, 119.71, 120.66, 125.03, 128.79, 129.05, 130.67, 131.82, 138.35, 140.49, 143.76, 145.12, 146.10, 147.95, 149.57; MALDI-MS for C25H25N4O3 calculated 429.49, found 429.43.

Details of the synthesis followed by the spectral representation of the characterization techniques involving MALDI-MS (Fig. S8), NMR (Fig. S9 and S10) and FTIR (Fig. S7) spectral analysis have been provided in supplementary section and shown through Scheme 1 .

UV–visible absorption study was carried out to elucidate the binding interaction between BR-N and Hb. The experiment was initiated by taking 1 μM solution of Hb prepared in 7.2 CP buffer in the 1 mL quartz cuvette and there into adding increasing amount of probe solution. The spectra were taken from 190 nm to 600 nm range with a fixed scan speed. During titration probe solutions were added in equivalent amount in the auxiliary cell prior to recording each spectrum for selectively considering the impact of probe solution on Hb spectra. Absorbance data were noted until saturation was met.

The sensing nature of the product was diagnosed thorough fluorescence study. Here initially 100 μM probe solution was poured into the fluorescence cell and the emission was noted in the range from 330 nm to 575 nm keeping the excitation wavelength fixed at 310 nm.The analyses were executed maintaining fixed scan speed of 600 nm/min with respective excitation and emission bandwidths of 2.5 nm and 5 nm. Firstly, the inherent fluorescence of BR-N was measured by placing only the probe solution into the cell and then the subsequent spectra were taken by gradual addition of Hb solution until the saturation condition was reached.

Inner filter effect is a serious issue when measuring fluorescence emission of a probe. It appears when the added compound has significant absorbance intensity at the excitation or emission wavelength. When it has absorbance at the excitation wavelength it allows lesser amount of light to excite the probe. Again, it passes lesser amount of light to reach to the fluorescence detector while absorbs emitted light for having absorbance at the wavelength noted to observe the emission. Thus it results in spurious reduction in the fluorescence emission intensity and thereby causes error in the analysis procedure [40]. Here, during fluorescence quenching experiment this discrepancy had been removed by the following equation to obtain the exact value of emission intensity:
F corr
=
F obs
×
e
A ex
+
A em
2
where the corrected value and observed value of fluorescence intensity are respectively represented by F corr and F obs and the absorbance at excitation and emission wavelengths are denoted by A ex and A em respectively.

The FT-IR spectral study was employed to unravel the existing surface functional groups in our synthesised probe and the variation in native conformation of Hb as a result of addition of growing concentration of probe solution. The presence of functional groups was ascertained by taking the spectra with small amount of the solid sample. However, to facilitate the elucidation of the conformational alternation of Hb a few more different spectra were recorded with 1000 μM free Hb and Hb-probe solution mixture respectively comprising of 50, 100 and 150 μM probe solution.

Quantum yield values (Φ) of the probe with and without the presence of Hb were achieved by taking optically identic solutions of quinine sulfate in 1(N) H2SO4 (Φr = 0.546) at excitation wavelength of 365 nm as standard solution and considering the equation written below [41]:
Φ s
=
Φ r
A r
F s
η s 2
/
A s
F r
η r 2
where A denotes the absorbance, F stands for the area integration of fluorescence intensities at the same wavelength of excitation, η is the solvents refractive index and the r and s subscripts are the reference and sample solutions respectively.

In time-resolved fluorescence experiment lifetime of the probes were estimated through the inbuilt Horiba EZ time software unit by fitting lifetime fluorescence intensity decay curves to the equation given below [42].
R
t
=
∑
i = 1
n
α i
exp
−
t
τ i

Where, αi and τi respectively signify the pre-exponential factors and relative lifetime of component i, n symbolizes as the number related to discrete decay components. The calculation to obtain the mean lifetimes (τm) was done by following the equation [43]:
τ m
=
α 1
τ 1
+
α 2
τ 2
+
α 3
τ 3

Samples of human urine were collected from five willing and healthy adult volunteers of our laboratory. At the beginning of this determination procedure, the urine samples were allowed to centrifuge at 4000 rpm with time-limit set at 10 min. Then only the supernatant portions of the samples were taken and considered for further analysis. They were subjected to 100-fold dilution adding solution of CP buffer (10 mM, pH 7.2) and finally the samples were ready for real sample analytical procedure.

UV–vis absorption study was performed to monitor the changes associated with absorbance spectrum of Hb in presence of our synthesised probe to observe the nature of interaction. Primarily, in absence of probe solution, the native spectra of Hb contained two peaks one at 404 nm (soret band) and the other centred at 195 nm due to π → π* transition of >C=O group. While the continuous addition of probe solution brought about gradual decrement of the absorbance intensity for both the inherent peaks of Hb. The absorbance spectral effect of adding the probe solution into Hb has been represented through Fig. 1 , where the inset image highlights the impact of probe addition on the soret band. Such behaviour of hypochromic shift in the peaks of Hb clearly points towards the interaction of the probe with Hb. Besides, the continuous reduction in the absorbance intensity of the soret band of Hb with progressive addition of the probe unambiguously indicated the occurrence of static quenching through ground state complex formation [6], as it is well-established that during spectrophotometric analysis the absorbance value remains constant in case of dynamic quenching while clearly changes for static quenching process [44]. Next, we move forward to find out the binding affinity value by analysing the absorbance data through the Benesi−Hildebrand equation [45]:
1
∆ A
=
1
∆ A
max
+
1
K BH
∆ A
max
×
1
Q

Where the Benesi−Hildebrand binding constant is expressed by K
BH and [Q] is the concentration of Hb (quencher). The plot turned out to be a linear one while fitted in the above equation (Fig. S1) and the binding affinity value ( K
BH ) found to be 2.8 × 105 M−1. Such a kind of high value of K
BH simply points towards the fact that the probe BR-N has strong binding affinity for Hb [46].

The fluorescence property of the probe was initially checked by performing three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence study. The probe was illuminated with different excitation wavelength starting from 250 nm to 400 nm and the emission intensity was noticed from 300 nm to 650 nm region keeping the bandwidth fixed at 2.5 nm. The results were presented through 3D spectra and corresponding contour maps in Fig. 2 , where the intensity of fluorescence, excitation and emission wavelength were reflected through the axes. In the figure, ‘peak a’ referred the peak for Rayleigh scattering of first-order (λex = λem) whereas ‘peak b’ referred the peak for Rayleigh scattering of second-order (λem = 2λex). Both the 3D spectra and its consequent contour fluorescence diagram confirmed that the probe became most fluorescent giving highest intensity (peak 1), when the wavelength of excitation was 310 nm. Further, the experiment was extended to measure the effect of Hb addition to the probe solution and observed a huge reduction in the highly elevated peak (peak 1), which supports the quenching of probe fluorescence by Hb.

Fluorescence emission study was adapted to demonstrate the sensing response of the synthesised probe. The probe showed very high emission intensity at 386 nm wavelength when a 100 μM solution of it in 7.2 CP buffer was excited with 310 nm wavelength. But the highly elevated intrinsic fluorescence intensity started to decrease rapidly with simultaneous addition of Hb in its solution. The emission intensity continued to get reduced until 12 μM Hb solutions was added leading to the saturation state. The addition caused a 6 fold lowering of the fluorescence intensity that lasted almost invariant even after 180 min. Again, it caused significant alternation in the nature of the intrinsic fluorescence curve of the probe resulting in a complete turn-off fluorescence effect (Fig. 3A). The emission intensity at 386 nm was related to the Hb concentration by the equation: Y = 4.02189*X+ 1.00048, where Y and X respectively represents the emission intensity at 386 nm and Hb concentration. Moreover, the linearity plot showed that the reduction of emission intensity was linear in the range of 1–70 nM with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9998 (Fig. 3B). As a result, when the detection limit was evaluated following the conventional IUPAC rule [CDL = 3σ /k, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, k is the slope between fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and concentration of Hb], it gave interestingly lower value. Thus, after obtaining the respective values the LOD value was found to be 0.41 nM that was lower than most of the already reported spectrometric approach till date (Table 1 ) [47–54]. Such an exclusive sensitivity of the probe towards quantitative identification of Hb can simply be envisioned through naked eye by illuminating with UV light of 366 nm wavelength inside an ultraviolet cabinet (inset image of Fig. 3A). Furthermore, the quantum yield evaluation of the probe before and after the addition of Hb showed significant fall in its value from 11% to 2% which was also consistent with the lowering of fluorescence intensity of the probe in adherence to Hb.

This experiment was considered to discover the pH dependency of the probe. The fluorescence intensity was measured preparing 100 μM probe solution of varying pH value from 3.0 to 11.0 (Fig. S2). It revealed that the emission intensity remained almost intact in response to the different pH solution with minor increment in the intensity in accordance with increase in the pH value which made it workable in a broad range of pH. So, the whole experiment was carried out using the biologically relevant CP buffer solution of 7.2 pH. Furthermore, the same experimental method was repeated to observe the effect on the probe-protein complex by adding Hb into the probe solution. But here also no significant alternation was noticed indicating its pH independent characteristics.

The probe was subjected to spectrofluorometric analysis over a long period of time with continuous irradiation under excitation wavelength of 310 nm to examine its photo stability. The fluorescence emission intensity shown by the probe was found to suffer negligible alternation even after 180 min (Fig. S3). The nearly similar value of fluorescence intensity throughout the extended time interval unambiguously reflected the high photo stability of the synthesised probe at room temperature. Again, the reduced fluorescence intensity due to addition of Hb remained almost unaltered beyond 180 min clearly revealing the stability of the fluorescence property of the probe-protein complex.

Selectivity study was performed by measuring the change in the fluorescence intensity of the probe (100 μM) in presence of various biologically similar compounds like glycine, alanine, histidine, methionine, proline, serine, TRP, tyrosine, valine, lysozyme, glutathione, ascorbic acid, ATP, cysteine, cystamine, EDTA, glucose, arabinose, galactose, myoglobin with equivalent experimental parameters. But none of the aforesaid biomolecules could cause any significant effect in the emission intensity of the probe (Fig. 4A and B). It was only myoglobin that brought about small quenching which was reasonable considering the presence of only one heme subunit in its structure compared to four heme subunits of Hb. However, the minor effect of myoglobin could be neglected while detecting Hb as its concentration in blood is much lower than that of Hb [55]. Again, since albumins have strong influence on the fluorescence properties of berberine and its derivatives the interference effect on Hb determination was also verified as albumins may be present in many samples of interest. But, in spite of the presence of sufficient amount of both bovine serum albumin (BSA) and human serum albumin (HSA) the probe was found to distinguish Hb while the albumins were failure to cause any significant change in the inherently elevated emission intensity of the probe (Fig. S4). Moreover, when the probe was subjected to the competitive experiment in presence of different available ions no momentous interference was perceived from any of them (Fig. S5). Thus the probe showed high selectivity in detecting Hb aptly.

The sensing nature of the probe was brought under light by adopting time resolved fluorescence decay measurements analysis. The probe when excited with 288 nm lamp exhibited bi-exponential decay components consisting fluorescence lifetime 1.21 ns (0.49%) and 4.47 ns (0.51%) producing average lifetime (τm) of 2.87 ns. While the addition of Hb in the probe solution turned out bi-exponential decay comprising fluorescence lifetime of 1.13 ns (0.50%) and 4.44 ns (0.50%) resulting average lifetime (τm) of 2.78 ns (Fig. 5 ). Thus it was clear that the fluorophoric probe kept its fluorescence lifetime almost constant before and after the addition of the quencher Hb. Such type of small changes occurring in the fluorescence lifetime of the probe unequivocally pointed towards static quenching of the fluorophore as a consequence of ground state complex formation. Thus only the non-complexed fluorophores should cause the net observed fluorescence as a portion of the fluorophores get eliminated from fluorescence measurement due to static quenching and the complexed fluorophores become non-fluorescent. Contrarily, the non-complexed part being undisturbed, and so the ratio of the average lifetime before and after the addition of Hb remains almost equal to 1, as already discussed in the previous articles for static quenching [56]. Thus, the quenching mechanism is certainly corroborated to follow static quenching pathway through complex formation in the ground state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational change of Hb using FTIR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The conformational variation of Hb caused by the addition of the probe was monitored with the aid of FTIR and the corresponding derivative method. Initially the IR spectrum of Hb showed two representative acid band amide-I (at 1653 cm−1) and amide-II (at 1545 cm−1) respectively corresponding to CO stretching vibration of globular peptide linkage and in plane NH bending vibration of peptide chain [57]. Though, both of these two bands properly reflects the conformation of the protein, but in quantitative study involving the aggregation, unfolding and denaturation of proteins only amide-I band is regarded as the forecaster globally due to very complex correlation on the secondary structure of amide-II band shape [58,59]. The absorption related to apiece secondary structure may be categorized considering the wavenumber of different sub-peaks such as, the absorption value associated with the β-sheet structure in the range 1610–1640 cm−1; the absorption value associated with random coil in the range 1641–1648 cm−1 structure; the absorption value corresponding to the α-helix structure in the range 1650–1660 cm−1; the absorption value related to the β-turn structure in the range 1660–1680 cm−1 and the absorption value for β-anti structure in the range 1680–1692 cm−1 [60]. The respective IR spectra and its deconvoluted spectra of amide-I band have been outlined through Fig. 6 . The contents of the secondary structures were calculated according to integral area of sub-peaks.The results are listed in Table S1, which revealed that the inherent α-helix content of the Hb moiety suffered gradual diminution with simultaneous augmentation of the probe at the expense of enhancement of other secondary structure contents. Such a tendency of steady reduction in its α-helix content indicated the probe-Hb binding interaction resulting into the conformational alternation in its secondary structure.

The probe-Hb binding stoichiometry was demonstrated with the help of Job's plot analysis method. Here, a total of 10 sets of solution mixtures were prepared by taking the probe and Hb solution in different ratios and the fluorescence emission intensity at fixed wavelength of 386 nm were considered. The outcome of this experiment was expressed by plotting mole-fraction of Hb (XHb) against ΔI × XHb which showed that only the solution mixture with 0.5 mol-fraction value of Hb was most efficient to provide maximum fluorescent intensity suggesting the existence of 1:1 binding stoichiometry in the probe-Hb complex (Fig. S6).

High fluorescence of the probe was put under investigation over an extended region of temperature from 20 to 80 °C keeping all other experimental parameters equivalent. The probe showed higher fluorescence intensity in the lower temperature region but thereafter endured monotonous fall in its emission intensity when the temperature was raised (Fig. 7A). The decrement in fluorescence intensity stayed linear in the whole range of temperature with 0.998 correlation coefficient (R2) (Fig. 7B). Further, the temperature augmentation did not fluctuate the emission wavelength (λmax) to a much extent (Fig. 7C). The decrease in intensity is referred to the activation of different non-radiative pathways caused by the elevated temperature. Moreover, the survey had been explored by recycling the probe solution temperature in between 20 °C and 80 °C repetitively up to 4 cycles only to check the reversibility and restorability of the probe fluorescence (Fig. 7D). It was found that the simultaneous effect of heating and cooling did not produce any noticeable alternation in the final intensity of the probe. It made us to conclude that the repetitive temperature change cannot bring about any kind of permanent demolition in the structure of the as-synthesised probe. Thus, the probe becomes a promising and attractive sensor for its fully recoverable and reversible temperature sensitive fluorescence application.

The research work had been explored to ensure the practicality and the reliability of the synthesised probe in quantitative recognition of Hb in real samples like human urine. A well-measured amount of Hb was spiked with the corresponding urine sample and the recovery value was verified in each case to confirm the feasibility of the highly fluorescent probe in clinical purpose. The out comings and the results of the real sample analysis were tabulated in Table 2 , where it was found that the percentile of recovery in the whole experimental procedure was in the range from 97.1% to 103.4%. Such enviable recoveries encourage us in the field of clinical application as it is an essential parameter for method validation, an indication of an almost complete mass balance of the recovery standard i.e. complete extraction with minimal losses and good alignment between spiking and calibration solution. Again, in the clinical field, such exciting recovery in the determined amount of Hb not only helps in predictability but also expresses the precision and accuracy of the analytical method and the efficiency of extraction of Hb from a sample with matrix. Thus, the experiment explicitly justifies the practical clinical usability of our synthesised probe as a fluorometric Hb recogniser in real sample application.

Through this research work, we have tried to establish a new 9-N-substituted berberine analogue as a prompt and effective, water soluble, novel sensor that can detect the presence of nanomolar concentration of Hb. The synthetic procedure undertaken herein is simple, single-step, time saving, cost effective, non-toxic and non-hazardous. The structure of the synthesised probe was confirmed by using mass, NMR and FT-IR spectroscopic technique. In spectrofluorimetric analysis method, the probe was found to have a huge value of intrinsic fluorescence intensity. The emission property was almost invariant in workable pH range from 3 to 11 and exhibited high photo stability over an extended range of time. Furthermore, temperature dependent fluorescence analysis proved that the probe could be applied as a promising and attractive sensor through its fully recoverable and reversible temperature sensitive fluorescence property. The probe was allowed to interact with a number of available relative biological compounds and almost all the known ions with intension of sensing any of them by causing any shift in its emission spectrum. But it was only Hb that selectively produced dramatic alternation in the spectrum through a large quenching of the fluorescence intensity and thereby causing significant ‘turn-off’ fluorescence emission. UV–visible absorbance study and time dependent fluorescence spectroscopy established that ground state probe-protein complex formation was behind the quenching mechanism to follow the static quenching pathway. The interaction study unearthed the binding constant value to be 2.8 × 105 M−1. FT-IR spectrometry and Job's plot analysis were the tools that were utilised to have an elaborate demonstration of the probe-protein binding interaction.The sensitivity of the probe was such that it recognized the presence of Hb with a very low value of detection limit of 0.41 nM having linearity in the range from 1 nM to 70 nM concentration of Hb. What is more, this method can also be successfully applied in case of Hb level determination in real human urine samples which speaks for its practical utility with significant recovery percentile. To conclude, it can be emphasized that, this technique has the potentiality to be one of the topmost pioneers in the field of nanomolar level quantified detection of Hb.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Sk Nayim acknowledges CSIR for Junior Research Fellowship (09/599(0089)/2019-EMR-I). Mt. Nasima Aktara appreciatively acknowledges UGC for MANF Fellowship. We also gratefully acknowledge the USIC, Vidyasagar University for their instrumental facilities and DST-FIST and UGC-SAP programs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2020.114741.

M.F. Perutz, X-ray Analysis of Hemoglobin, Science 140 (1963) 863-869.
